flow 1: api -> pages -> repository / controllers -> models -> controllers -> mysql -> controllers -> bunny.io -> controllers -> jobs -> tag signal -> controllers -> real-time communication service -> controllers -> push notifications system
the diagram depicts a system architecture with an api serving as the entry point for user interface pages.
data is managed by repository/controllers, which interacts with models, controllers, and a mysql database.
bunny.io is used for content delivery, and jobs handle background tasks, potentially triggering tag signals.
real-time communication and push notifications are integrated into the system.
the main flow involves a sequence of interactions between components to handle requests and manage data.

jobs -> migrations — type=association; label=""; explanation="jobs are related to migrations"
utente -> app — type=message; label="compilazione campi"; explanation="user provides input"
app -> server — type=message; label="doregistration()"; explanation="application calls registration function"
server -> database — type=message; label="salvataggio dati"; explanation="server saves data"
database -> server — type=message; label="conferma inserimento"; explanation="database confirms data insertion"
server -> server — type=message; label="generateotp()"; explanation="server generates otp"
server -> database — type=message; label="salvataggio otp"; explanation="server saves otp"
database -> server — type=message; label="conferma inserimento"; explanation="database confirms otp insertion"
utente -> app — type=message; label="digita codice otp"; explanation="user enters otp"
app -> server — type=message; label="sendotp()"; explanation="application sends otp"
server -> database — type=message; label="chiede otp salvato"; explanation="server requests saved otp"
database -> server — type=message; label="riceve otp salvato"; explanation="database provides saved otp"
server -> server — type=message; label="\$check = checkotp()"; explanation="server checks otp"
server -> app — type=message; label="[\\$check]: seridauthtoken()"; explanation="server returns authentication token"

flow 1: utente -> app (compilazione campi) -> server (doregistration()) -> database (salvataggio dati) -> server (conferma inserimento) -> server (generateotp()) -> database (salvataggio otp) -> server (conferma inserimento) -> utente (digita codice otp) -> app (sendotp()) -> server (chiede otp salvato) -> database (riceve otp salvato) -> server (\$check = checkotp()) -> app ([$check]: seridauthtoken())
the diagram models a user registration process.
the user provides input to the application, which then calls the server's registration function.
the server saves the user's data and generates an otp, which is also saved.
the user enters the otp, which is then verified against the saved otp.
upon successful verification, the server returns an authentication token to the application.
the diagram shows a linear flow without any branching or looping.
app -> server — type=message; label="schecku: emailvalidate()"; explanation="app calls email validation"
app -> server — type=message; label="scheckp: passwordvalidate()"; explanation="app calls password validation"
server -> server — type=message; label="[schecku and scheckp]: dologin()"; explanation="server initiates login process"
server -> database — type=message; label="richiesta password associata alla mail"; explanation="server requests password from database"
database -> server — type=message; label="invio password"; explanation="database returns password"
server -> server — type=message; label="scheck = checkpassword()"; explanation="server checks password"
server -> server — type=message; label="[scheck]: generatetoken()"; explanation="server generates authentication token"
server -> app — type=message; label="[scheck]: sendauthtoken()"; explanation="server sends authentication token to app"
app -> utente — type=message; label="salvataggio authtoken()"; explanation="app saves authentication token"
app -> utente — type=message; label="apertura schermata home"; explanation="app opens home screen"

flow 1: utente -> app (compilazione campi) -> server (emailvalidate()) -> server (passwordvalidate()) -> server (dologin()) -> database (richiesta password associata alla mail) -> server (invio password) -> server (checkpassword()) -> server (generatetoken()) -> app (sendauthtoken()) -> app (salvataggio authtoken()) -> app (apertura schermata home)
the diagram models a login sequence involving a user, an application, a server, and a database.
the user provides credentials to the application, which forwards them to the server.

the server validates the email and password, retrieves the password from the database, checks the provided password against the stored password, generates an authentication token, and sends it back to the application.
the application saves the token and opens the home screen for the user.
the diagram does not explicitly show error handling or alternative flows for invalid credentials.

server -> database — type=message; label="salva nuova pwd"; explanation="server saves new password"
database -> server — type=message; label="conferma nuova psw"; explanation="database confirms new password saving"
server -> utente — type=message; label="comunicazione cambio password avvenuta"; explanation="server informs user of password change"

flow 1: utente -> app (richiesta psw dimenticata) -> server (lostpswrequest()) -> database (check mail) -> server (genera token recupero psw) -> database (conferma salvataggio) -> app (mail con link) -> utente (invio password & token recupero psw) -> server (checktokenpsw()) -> database (conferma nuova psw) -> server (comunicazione cambio password avvenuta)
the diagram models a password recovery process.
the flow starts with a user request and ends with confirmation of the password change.
a key branch occurs when the email check fails, leading to token generation and email sending.
the process involves the user, app, server, and database components.
the token validation step is crucial for security.

the diagram models a sequence of interactions for a card-based application, likely a dating or recommendation app.
the user initially opens the homepage, which triggers the application to request and display a set of cards.
subsequent swipes by the user trigger requests for more cards, with an offset to ensure a continuous stream of new content.
the server acts as an intermediary between the application and the database, handling data requests and responses.

the diagram models a message sending process between two users (utente1 and utente2) using two applications (app1 and app2) and a server (server) with a database (database).
user 1 enters a message, which is sent to the server, saved in the database, and a notification is sent to user 2.
user 2 receives the notification, opens the chat, and retrieves the message from the server, which is then displayed.
the process involves token-based authentication ([token1] and [token2]) for sending and retrieving messages.

server -> app utente 2: "chiede se ci sono match" -> [true in questo caso]
